data.title: Shift away from EVs continues to cost Avis Budget
data.description:
Shares of Avis Budget Group (CAR) were underwater in Wednesday’s after-hours trading as fourth-quarter results missed expectations amid the company’s efforts to reposition the business, including another charge to reduce the carrying value of its U.S. EV fleet.

“As we enter 2026, we’ve repositioned the business and turned a challenging fourth quarter into a catalyst for meaningful change,” said Brian Choi, Avis Budget Group CEO. “We are tightening fleet discipline, strengthening our balance sheet, and raising the bar on customer experience to drive sustainable earnings growth.”

Sales during the quarter were down 0.4% to a below consensus $2.7B as rental days fell 1% compared to the same quarter last year.

Profitability improved, however, with the company’s net loss narrowing by 56% to ($856M) and to ($21.25) on a per share basis, although this was still significantly worse than expectations for a loss of just $0.38 per share.

Adjusted EBITDA in the Americas improved to $1M from a loss of $63M last year, as lower fleet costs were partially offset by a 4% decrease in revenue per day.

Combined vehicle utilization (Americas and International) improved by 30 basis points to 68.0%, while per unit fleet costs per month were down 18%.

data.title: Gold and silver prices rebound on bargain buying, geopolitical fears
data.description:
Gold and silver futures rebounded Wednesday as investors bought the dip after a two-day decline, but prices remain well below the respective record-high settlements of $5,318.40/oz and $115.08/oz that were reached earlier this year.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for February delivery closed +2.1% to $4,986.50/oz, and front-month Comex February silver (XAGUSD:CUR) finished +5.5% to $77.509/oz.

On the geopolitical front, the first day of Russia-Ukraine peace talks in Geneva ended after just two hours, with Ukraine's President Zelenskiy describing the discussions as "difficult" and accusing the Russians of stalling.

A day after Iran's foreign minister said nuclear talks were "constructive," Vice President Vance said Iran has not addressed U.S. red lines in the discussions and that President Trump reserves the right to use military force.

Axios reported the Trump administration is "closer to a major war in the Middle East than most Americans realize," which "could begin very soon."

Iran and Russia reportedly will conduct navy drills in the Sea of Oman and the northern Indian Ocean on Thursday, after Iran's Revolutionary Guards conducted drills in the Strait of Hormuz earlier this week.

"There is some nervousness about the existing geopolitical tensions, [but] we've been in a very tight trading range for much of February," Marex analyst Edward Meir said in a note. "You can't really say there’s a clear direction at this point."

After regular Comex trading ended for the day, the Federal Reserve released the minutes of its January 27-28 policy meeting, which indicated Fed officials showed little inclination to reduce interest rates, with most members preferring to see more progress on inflation before considering any further cuts.

Even as two officials opposed the decision to hold the benchmark short-term rate steady in a 3.5%-3.75% range and favored a cut, the minutes showed that other officials would have supported more neutral language characterizing the prospect of a rate cut or a rate hike as evenly balanced.

With much of Asian trading offline because of the Lunar New Year holiday, investors "can reasonably expect a soft patch" for precious metals, opening a window for bargain-buying, BMO Capital analysts said in a note.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Fed sees little refi impact from Trump's push into mortgage debt
data.description:
Minutes from the Federal Reserve's January meeting indicate that the Trump administration's plan to lower borrowing costs by buying mortgage bonds may not produce a meaningful pickup in refinancing activity, even after the recent retreat in mortgage rates.

Last month, U.S. President Donald Trump directed Fannie Mae (FNMA) and Freddie Mac (FMCC) to buy $200B of mortgage debt as part of the administration's broader push to make homeownership more affordable, though the move received investor pushback

"The recent announcement that Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac may increase their mortgage investment portfolios garnered substantial market attention and was followed by a notable decline in mortgage-backed securities yields relative to those on comparable-maturity Treasury yields," the FOMC minutes said. 

"Still, the manager observed that the decline was unlikely to result in a material increase in mortgage refinancing because current mortgage rates are well above the weighted average rate of outstanding mortgages."

Though still elevated, mortgage rates have improved markedly from a year ago, with the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage averaging 6.09% on Feb. 12 vs. 6.87% a year earlier. Meanwhile, the Fed paused its recent rate-cutting streak in January as inflation remains slightly above target.

Real Estate ETFs: (MBB), (VNQ), (XLRE), (IYR), (USRT), and (HOMZ).

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
